items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: Why is burning fossil fuels considered to disrupt the carbon cycle?
items.correct: It releases carbon stored for millions of years much faster than the cycle can absorb.
items.misconception: It creates new carbon that did not exist before.
items.why: Students think combustion creates carbon. It RELEASES previously stored carbon - the issue is rate. Geological storage took millions of years; we're releasing it in centuries, overwhelming natural sinks.

items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: Marginal abatement cost curves rank options by:
items.why: Correct answer: Cost per tonne CO₂e abated. “Volume of emissions” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Reductions occur within the company's own value chain
items.why: It's Reductions occur within the company's own value chain. “It is cheaper but unverified” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If household emissions are 8 t CO₂e and switching to a heat pump cuts heating from 3 t to 0.5 t, total new emissions (t CO₂e) is?
items.why: The real figure is 5.5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
